steps.title: Identify the structure on each side
steps.content: Left: "Paintings by the renowned twentieth-century US" — an incomplete noun phrase needing its head noun. Right: "were featured in Artist to Artist..." — an independent clause. The blank supplies the noun plus an appositive naming the two specific artists.

steps.title: Apply the rule: restrictive appositive needs no commas
steps.content:
The names Thomas Hart Benton and Jackson Pollack identify which artists — they are essential, not extra information. A restrictive appositive takes no commas.
  • Choice A — comma after Benton, comma after Pollack: "artists Thomas Hart Benton, and Jackson Pollack," — the comma before and suggests a three-item list with a missing third item, and the trailing comma has nothing to close. ✗
  • Choice B — colon, no trailing comma: "artists: Thomas Hart Benton and Jackson Pollack," — a colon cannot separate a noun from its appositive, and the trailing comma is unmatched. ✗
  • Choice C — no punctuation: "artists Thomas Hart Benton and Jackson Pollack" — the names flow directly as a restrictive appositive; no punctuation needed. ✓
  • Choice D — comma after artists: "artists, Thomas Hart Benton and Jackson Pollack" — a comma before a restrictive appositive is wrong; only non-restrictive appositives take commas. ✗
That matches choice C — C is correct.
